docs.title: Welcome to the Blog
docs.text: This is the first post. A good place to introduce yourself, explain what you'll be writing about, and set expectations for readers.

docs.title: What This Blog Is About
docs.text: Write a few sentences about the topics you'll cover — tools, techniques, war stories, opinions on the industry.

docs.title: What to Expect
docs.text:
• How often you'll post
  • What kind of content (tutorials, research, rants)
  • How readers can reach you

More soon.

docs.title: Post Title Goes Here
docs.text: A one-sentence hook that makes the reader want to keep going.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Context for the post. What problem are you solving, what got you interested in this topic?

docs.title: The Meat of It
docs.text:
Write the main content here. Use code blocks, screenshots, and diagrams to support your points.

# Example command
nmap -sV -sC -p- target.example.com

docs.title: Takeaways
docs.text:
• Bullet summary of key points
  • What readers should do with this information

docs.title: References
docs.text: • Link to tool or paper

docs.title: Hack Back
docs.text:
In 2024, I developed Hack Back, a real-time strategy game where you play through a fun campaign as an Offensive Security Engineer. Inspired by StarCraft, you control various heroes and units to launch network attacks against MegaCorp bad guys while defending your own base from counterstrikes.

The game incorporates cybersecurity principles into engaging gameplay mechanics to teach the world about offensive and defensive security concepts in a simple way.

docs.title: Gameplay
docs.text:
• Real-time strategy — control units, manage resources, build your base, and decide when to strike, stay stealthy, or fall back
  • Campaign-driven narrative — follow a protagonist who joins a security consulting firm and gets pulled into escalating conflict
  • Accessible by design — hacking mechanics are woven into gameplay rather than dumped in a terminal, so anyone can pick it up

docs.title: Current State
docs.text:
I released the first two levels of the game for free on Steam. I currently don't have plans of continuing development past these initial two levels. Last I checked, there were about 14k downloads.

Free Download on Steam

docs.title: Red Team Strategy Guide
docs.text: Thoughtfully approaching red teaming within large organizations to maximize business impact.

docs.title: About This Guide
docs.text: The Red Team Strategy Guide (RTSG) is for current and aspiring red team leaders who want to maximize their impact. Red teaming in big companies is ambiguous work, and it's hard to nail down what really matters and actually makes a difference. This guide is not another collection of technical tradecrafts or secret TTPs, rather it provides strategic insight for leaders seeking to build and mature effective red team programs that influence meaningful security improvements across their organizations. This is the "impact" I expound on throughout this guide.

docs.title: Who This Is For
docs.text:
• Red team leaders who want their operations to drive real security improvements, not just generate reports
  • Senior red teamers looking to grow beyond technical execution into program strategy and leadership
  • Junior red teamers who want to understand the bigger picture early and avoid spending years figuring out what actually matters
  • Security leaders evaluating how to build or mature an internal red team program

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text: Each section stands alone. Jump to whatever's relevant. If you're new to red teaming, start at the beginning.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Have something to add? Please read through the Introduction, and if your proposed content aligns with the intent of this guide, then open an Issue or a Pull Request.

docs.title: Chapter 1: Introduction
docs.text: What this chapter covers and why it matters — one sentence.

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: Explain what problem this book (and this chapter) is addressing. Make the reader feel the pain before you offer the solution.

Personal Story

When I was a young lad...

docs.title: A Brief History
docs.text: Optional — but often useful for grounding readers. How did we get here? What failed or changed that makes this topic relevant now?

docs.title: What You'll Learn in This Book
docs.text:
A short roadmap of the chapters and how they connect. Give readers a reason to keep going.

docs.title: Chapter 2: Core Concepts
docs.text: The foundational ideas everything else builds on.

docs.title: Concept One
docs.text:
Explain the first core concept. Use examples. Show, don't just tell.

# Code example if applicable
def example():
    pass

docs.title: Concept Two
docs.text: Second concept. How does it relate to or build on the first?

docs.title: A Sub-Point
docs.text: Go deeper when needed. Use subheadings to break up long sections, not to artificially inflate structure.

docs.title: Concept Three
docs.text: Third concept. By now you should be building toward something — a framework, a model, a way of thinking.

docs.title: Putting It Together
docs.text:
How do these concepts interact? Walk through a worked example that combines all three.

Note

A note or aside that's useful but would interrupt the flow of the main text.

docs.title: Chapter 3: Advanced Topics
docs.text: For readers who've mastered the basics and want to go further.

docs.title: Advanced Topic One
docs.text: This chapter assumes you've read Chapters 1 and 2. Reference back when needed to avoid re-explaining fundamentals.

docs.title: Deep Dive
docs.text: Go as deep as the content requires here. This is the place for nuance, edge cases, and the things that don't fit neatly into the earlier framework.

docs.title: Advanced Topic Two
docs.text:
Another advanced subject. Consider using comparison tables, diagrams, or detailed walkthroughs.

Approach Pros Cons Method A Fast, simple Limited flexibility Method B Flexible More complex Method C Best of both Requires X

docs.title: Edge Cases and Gotchas
docs.text:
A section on things that trip people up. This is often the most valuable part of an advanced chapter.

Common Mistake

Describe the mistake and why it happens. This kind of callout helps readers recognize the pitfall before they hit it.
